papers.title: Coordinated Behavior on Social Media in 2019 UK General Election
papers.abstract: Coordinated online behaviors are an essential part of information and influence operations, as they allow a more effective disinformation's spread. Most studies on coordinated behaviors involved manual investigations, and the few existing computational approaches make bold assumptions or oversimplify the problem to make it tractable. Here, we propose a new network-based framework for uncovering and studying coordinated behaviors on social media. Our research extends existing systems and goes beyond limiting binary classifications of coordinated and uncoordinated behaviors. It allows to expose different coordination patterns and to estimate the degree of coordination that characterizes diverse communities. We apply our framework to a dataset collected during the 2019 UK General Election, detecting and characterizing coordinated communities that participated in the electoral debate. Our work conveys both theoretical and practical implications and provides more nuanced and fine-grained results for studying online information manipulation.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on extracting social determinants of health from clinical notes using NLP. There is no methodological overlap in terms of NLP techniques, model architectures (BERT vs. network-based framework), or application domains (clinical notes vs. social media coordination detection). | Domain: The user's research idea is in the clinical NLP domain, focusing on extracting social determinants of health from EHR notes. papers.title: Leveraging Natural Language Processing to Augment Structured Social Determinants of Health Data in the Electronic Health Record
papers.abstract:
Objective: Social determinants of health (SDOH) impact health outcomes and are documented in the electronic health record (EHR) through structured data and unstructured clinical notes. However, clinical notes often contain more comprehensive SDOH information, detailing aspects such as status, severity, and temporality. This work has two primary objectives: i) develop a natural language processing (NLP) information extraction model to capture detailed SDOH information and ii) evaluate the information gain achieved by applying the SDOH extractor to clinical narratives and combining the extracted representations with existing structured data.
  Materials and Methods: We developed a novel SDOH extractor using a deep learning entity and relation extraction architecture to characterize SDOH across various dimensions. In an EHR case study, we applied the SDOH extractor to a large clinical data set with 225,089 patients and 430,406 notes with social history sections and compared the extracted SDOH information with existing structured data.
  Results: The SDOH extractor achieved 0.86 F1 on a withheld test set. In the EHR case study, we found extracted SDOH information complements existing structured data with 32% of homeless patients, 19% of current tobacco users, and 10% of drug users only having these health risk factors documented in the clinical narrative.
  Conclusions: Utilizing EHR data to identify SDOH health risk factors and social needs may improve patient care and outcomes. Semantic representations of text-encoded SDOH information can augment existing structured data, and this more comprehensive SDOH representation can assist health systems in identifying and addressing these social needs.

Both explicitly aim to extract SDOH from clinical notes using NLP, with the paper specifically mentioning 'housing instability' (living status (homeless)) as one of the SDOH extracted, directly aligning with the user's idea. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper propose a clinical NLP pipeline for extracting social determinants of health from unstructured clinical notes in EHR systems. The user specifies a 'domain-adapted BERT model,' while the paper uses a 'deep learning entity and relation extraction architecture' and mentions 'mSpERT' (a BERT-based model for clinical text). The paper also mentions using a "deep learning entity and relation extraction architecture" which is a type of model that could include domain-adapted BERT. | Contribution: The paper's primary objective is to "develop a natural language processing (NLP) information extraction model to capture detailed SDOH information" and specifically mentions extracting 'housing instability' (referred to as 'Living Status (homeless)' in Table 5) from unstructured clinical notes. papers.title: Exploring Unknown Social Networks for Discovering Hidden Nodes
papers.abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of discovering hidden nodes in unknown social networks, formulating three types of hidden-node discovery problems, namely, Sybil-node discovery, peripheral-node discovery, and influencer discovery. We tackle these problems by employing a graph exploration framework grounded in machine learning. Leveraging the structure of the subgraph gradually obtained from graph exploration, we construct prediction models to identify target hidden nodes in unknown social graphs. Through empirical investigations of real social graphs, we investigate the efficiency of graph exploration strategies in uncovering hidden nodes. Our results show that our graph exploration strategies discover hidden nodes with an efficiency comparable to that when the graph structure is known. Specifically, the query cost of discovering 10% of the hidden nodes is at most only 1.2 times that when the topology is known, and the query-cost multiplier for discovering 90% of the hidden nodes is at most only 1.4. Furthermore, our results suggest that using node embeddings, which are low-dimensional vector representations of nodes, for hidden-node discovery is a double-edged sword: it is effective in certain scenarios but sometimes degrades the efficiency of node discovery. Guided by this observation, we examine the effectiveness of using a bandit algorithm to combine the prediction models that use node embeddings with those that do not, and our analysis shows that the bandit-based graph exploration strategy achieves efficient node discovery across a wide array of settings.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on extracting social determinants of health from clinical notes using NLP and BERT models. While both involve 'discovery' and machine learning, the specific domain (clinical notes vs. social networks) and the nature of the 'hidden' information (SDOH vs. network roles/maliciousness) are distinct, leading to different research questions and methodologies. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a clinical NLP pipeline using a domain-adapted BERT model for information extraction from unstructured clinical notes. The paper, however, focuses on graph exploration and machine learning models (including node embeddings and bandit algorithms) for discovering hidden nodes in social networks with unknown topologies. There is no methodological overlap in terms of NLP, BERT, clinical notes, or information extraction. | Domain: The user's idea is in clinical NLP for extracting social determinants of health from EHR notes, which is a healthcare application. In contrast, the paper addresses the discovery of hidden nodes (Sybil, peripheral, influencer) in unknown social networks using graph exploration and machine learning, which is a different domain and methodological approach. The paper does not mention clinical NLP, EHR systems, or social determinants of health.

papers.title: SynthEHR-Eviction: Enhancing Eviction SDoH Detection with LLM-Augmented Synthetic EHR Data
papers.abstract: Eviction is a significant yet understudied social determinants of health (SDoH), linked to housing instability, unemployment, and mental health. While eviction appears in unstructured electronic health records (EHRs), it is rarely coded in structured fields, limiting downstream applications. We introduce SynthEHR-Eviction, a scalable pipeline combining LLMs, human-in-the-loop annotation, and automated prompt optimization (APO) to extract eviction statuses from clinical notes. Using this pipeline, we created the largest public eviction-related SDoH dataset to date, comprising 14 fine-grained categories. Fine-tuned LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5, LLaMA3) trained on SynthEHR-Eviction achieved Macro-F1 scores of 88.8% (eviction) and 90.3% (other SDoH) on human validated data, outperforming GPT-4o-APO (87.8%, 87.3%), GPT-4o-mini-APO (69.1%, 78.1%), and BioBERT (60.7%, 68.3%), while enabling cost-effective deployment across various model sizes. The pipeline reduces annotation effort by over 80%, accelerates dataset creation, enables scalable eviction detection, and generalizes to other information extraction tasks.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses a very similar problem statement: extracting social determinants of health (SDoH) from unstructured clinical notes in EHR systems. The primary difference is the paper's specific focus on eviction and its use of LLMs for synthetic data generation, which is a method rather than a different problem. | Method: The user's idea proposes a clinical NLP pipeline using a domain-adapted BERT model. The paper describes a clinical NLP pipeline, but it primarily uses LLMs (Qwen2.5, LLaMA3) and LLM-augmented synthetic data, outperforming BioBERT (a BERT-like model). While both involve clinical NLP pipelines and extract SDoH from EHR notes, the core model architecture and data generation strategy differ significantly, with the paper focusing on LLMs and synthetic data generation rather than a domain-adapted BERT as the primary extraction model. | Domain: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea by focusing on a clinical NLP pipeline to extract social determinants of health (SDoH) from unstructured clinical notes in EHR systems. Specifically, it extracts 'eviction statuses' which are a form of housing instability, and mentions generalizability to 'food insecurity,' directly aligning with the user's specified SDoH categories and data source. | Contribution: The user's idea proposes a clinical NLP pipeline using a domain-adapted BERT model to extract social determinants of health (housing instability, food insecurity). The paper introduces 'SynthEHR-Eviction,' a pipeline that uses LLMs (outperforming BioBERT, a BERT-based model) to extract eviction statuses and other SDoH (including housing instability, which is a form of housing instability) from clinical notes. While both aim to extract SDoH from clinical notes using NLP, the paper's primary focus is on eviction and LLMs, whereas the user's idea specifies BERT and includes food insecurity, which is mentioned as a generalizable SDoH but not a primary focus of the paper's specific contributions.

papers.title: Dynamic Survival Transformers for Causal Inference with Electronic Health Records
papers.abstract: In medicine, researchers often seek to infer the effects of a given treatment on patients' outcomes. However, the standard methods for causal survival analysis make simplistic assumptions about the data-generating process and cannot capture complex interactions among patient covariates. We introduce the Dynamic Survival Transformer (DynST), a deep survival model that trains on electronic health records (EHRs). Unlike previous transformers used in survival analysis, DynST can make use of time-varying information to predict evolving survival probabilities. We derive a semi-synthetic EHR dataset from MIMIC-III to show that DynST can accurately estimate the causal effect of a treatment intervention on restricted mean survival time (RMST). We demonstrate that DynST achieves better predictive and causal estimation than two alternative models.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on extracting social determinants of health from unstructured clinical notes using NLP. In contrast, the paper addresses the problem of causal inference in survival analysis using EHR data to estimate treatment effects on time-to-event outcomes, which is a fundamentally different research question. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper utilize deep learning models, specifically Transformer architectures, for processing EHR data. However, their applications and specific model designs differ significantly: the user's idea focuses on an NLP pipeline with a domain-adapted BERT for information extraction (SDOH), while the paper proposes the Dynamic Survival Transformer (DynST) for causal inference and survival analysis. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper operate within the healthcare domain, specifically utilizing Electronic Health Records (EHRs). However, their application targets differ: the user's idea focuses on extracting social determinants of health using NLP, while the paper focuses on causal inference for treatment effects and survival analysis using transformer models on EHR data. | Contribution: The user's research idea focuses on extracting social determinants of health from unstructured clinical notes using a domain-adapted BERT model. In contrast, the paper introduces DynST, a deep survival model based on Transformers, to estimate causal treatment effects on survival outcomes from EHR data, including time-varying information. These are distinct contributions addressing different research gaps.

matched_sections.paper_title: SynthEHR-Eviction: Enhancing Eviction SDoH Detection with LLM-Augmented Synthetic EHR Data
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: While eviction appears in unstructured electronic health records (EHRs), it is rarely coded in structured fields, limiting downstream applications. We introduce SynthEHR-Eviction, a scalable pipeline combining LLMs, human-in-the-loop annotation, and automated prompt optimization (APO) to extract eviction statuses from clinical notes.
matched_sections.reason: The paper identifies the problem of extracting social determinants of health (SDoH), specifically eviction, from unstructured clinical notes in EHRs, which is very similar to the user's stated problem of extracting SDoH from unstructured clinical notes in EHR systems.

matched_sections.paper_title: SynthEHR-Eviction: Enhancing Eviction SDoH Detection with LLM-Augmented Synthetic EHR Data
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Yet, in current electronic health records (EHRs), SDoH information is often embedded within unstructured free-text clinical notes, lacking systematic, structured documentation 11 . This results in 'information silos', where crucial social risk factors are underutilized in real-world healthcare delivery, limiting clinicians' ability to assess patients' broader health risks and restricting healthcare institutions and policymakers from designing proactive, targeted intervention strategies 12 .
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states that SDoH information is embedded in unstructured clinical notes in EHRs, which aligns with the user's problem statement.

matched_sections.paper_title: SynthEHR-Eviction: Enhancing Eviction SDoH Detection with LLM-Augmented Synthetic EHR Data
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Fine-tuned LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5, LLaMA3) trained on SynthEHR-Eviction achieved Macro-F1 scores of 88.8% (eviction) and 90.3% (other SDoH) on human validated data, outperforming GPT-4o-APO (87.8%, 87.3%), GPT-4o-mini-APO (69.1%, 78.1%), and BioBERT (60.7%, 68.3%), while enabling cost-effective deployment across various model sizes.
matched_sections.reason: The user proposes a 'domain-adapted BERT model' while the paper uses 'fine-tuned LLMs' and mentions 'BioBERT' as a baseline. Both approaches involve adapting pre-trained language models for the clinical domain, but the paper focuses on LLMs beyond just BERT.

matched_sections.paper_title: SynthEHR-Eviction: Enhancing Eviction SDoH Detection with LLM-Augmented Synthetic EHR Data
matched_sections.heading: Discussion
matched_sections.text_snippet: On the challenging PMC multi-class classification task, a general-domain LLaMA-3.1-8B model achieved a Micro-F1 of 0.861, while the BERT-base-cased model fell to just 0.444-a difference of over 40 percentage points. This substantial gap underscores LLMs' superior ability to handle diverse and noisy clinical narratives, likely due to their larger parameter counts, broader pretraining corpora, and emergent reasoning capabilities 11 .

linked_sections.paper_title: Leveraging Natural Language Processing to Augment Structured Social Determinants of Health Data in the Electronic Health Record
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Objective: Social determinants of health (SDOH) impact health outcomes and are documented in the electronic health record (EHR) through structured data and unstructured clinical notes. However, clinical notes often contain more comprehensive SDOH information, detailing aspects such as status, severity, and temporality. This work has two primary objectives: i) develop a natural language processing (NLP) information extraction model to capture detailed SDOH information and ii) evaluate the information gain achieved by applying the SDOH extractor to clinical narratives and combining the extracted representations with existing structured data.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper aim to extract social determinants of health from unstructured clinical notes in EHR systems using NLP.

linked_sections.paper_title: Leveraging Natural Language Processing to Augment Structured Social Determinants of Health Data in the Electronic Health Record
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Materials and Methods: We developed a novel SDOH extractor using a deep learning entity and relation extraction architecture to characterize SDOH across various dimensions.
linked_sections.reason: The paper uses a deep learning entity and relation extraction architecture for SDOH extraction, which is a type of NLP model. While the user specifies 'domain-adapted BERT model', BERT is a deep learning model commonly used for entity and relation extraction in NLP, indicating a very similar methodological approach.

linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper operate within the domain of clinical NLP, specifically targeting the extraction of SDOH from unstructured clinical notes in EHR systems.

This work has two primary objectives: i) develop a natural language processing (NLP) information extraction model to capture detailed SDOH information and ii) evaluate the information gain achieved by applying the SDOH extractor to clinical narratives and combining the extracted representations with existing structured data.
